title: Charlie Ewels hit on Sexton — England vs Ireland 2022
explanation: Ewels makes direct contact with the head of Sexton with no mitigation. Upright tackle with clear head contact — straight red card under the Head Contact Process. No wrap, no dip, no sudden change of height by the ball carrier.

title: TMO confirms penalty try and Watson yellow — NatWest 6 Nations
explanation: The defending player commits a professional foul preventing a probable try. TMO reviews and confirms penalty try. Yellow card issued — the infringement was cynical and directly prevented a probable try.

title: Valetini head clash — red card incident
explanation: Head-on-head contact in the tackle. Under the Head Contact Process: direct contact with the head, high degree of danger. The tackler has a duty of care to tackle safely regardless of intent. Red card.

title: Kwagga Smith incident — South Africa vs Argentina 2022
explanation: Professional foul near the try line. The referee awards a penalty try — a try would probably have been scored but for the cynical infringement. Yellow card for the offending player.

title: Cheslin Kolbe challenge — South Africa vs Ireland 2022
explanation: Kolbe's challenge is reviewed. The officials assess degree of danger and mitigating factors under the Head Contact Process. Low force and passive movement reduce the sanction from red to yellow. This is a borderline call — good training for understanding mitigation.

title: Frisby penalty try — controversial call
explanation: The scrum-half's actions are deemed a professional foul preventing a probable try. Controversial because it required careful assessment of whether a try would probably have been scored without the infringement.

title: Damian de Allende red card incident
explanation: De Allende makes contact with the head of the ball carrier. Under the Head Contact Process: direct contact to the head with at least a medium degree of danger. Red card following the framework.

title: Yellow card chaos — All Blacks vs Scotland 2025
explanation: One of three yellow cards in a chaotic match at Murrayfield. Assess the degree of danger, whether there was direct contact with the head, and any mitigating factors before deciding on the appropriate sanction.

title: Breakdown penalty — All Blacks vs England 2025
explanation: The tackler fails to roll away from the ball after completing the tackle. This prevents the attacking team from using the ball quickly. Clear penalty — the tackler must make an immediate effort to move away.

title: Scrum penalty — All Blacks vs England 2025
explanation: The scrum collapses and the referee must determine fault. Look for hinging, angling in, boring, or early engagement. Scrum penalties are among the hardest calls — even experienced referees find these challenging.

title: Clean open-field tackle — All Blacks vs England 2025
explanation: A strong but perfectly legal tackle. Full wrap around the ball carrier's torso, below the shoulders, with both arms. Good technique — play on. Not every big hit is a penalty!

title: Ruck contest — All Blacks vs England 2025
explanation: A player contests at the breakdown but does so legally — enters through the gate, stays on their feet, and releases when the ruck forms. No infringement. Referees must distinguish between aggressive but legal play and actual penalties.

title: Jackal turnover — All Blacks vs England 2025
explanation: A legal jackal turnover at the breakdown. The defender stays on their feet, arrives before the ruck forms, and gets hands on the ball. This is excellent defensive play, not a penalty. Reward good technique by playing on.

title: Big legal tackle — no penalty
explanation: A massive but completely legal hit. The tackler wraps their arms, makes contact below the shoulders, and drives through. Big does not mean illegal — this is textbook tackling. Play on.

title: Hard but clean — dominant tackle
explanation: Another hard, clean tackle from this compilation. The impact looks severe but the technique is correct — arms wrapped, contact point below the line of the shoulders. No penalty. It's crucial for referees not to penalise physicality when the technique is legal.

title: Massive legal tackle example
explanation: RugbyPass example of a massive but legal tackle. The key elements: initial contact point is below the shoulders, tackler uses arms to wrap, ball carrier is driven back legally. Impressive physicality within the laws.

title: Women's International — England vs France 2024
explanation: A defensive player is ahead of the offside line in general play. The offside line at a ruck is the hindmost foot of the last player in the ruck. Penalty — the defender gained an unfair advantage by being in front of the offside line.

title: Women's International — clean contest at breakdown
explanation: A strong but legal contest at the breakdown in women's international rugby. The arriving player enters through the gate and competes for the ball on their feet. Legal play — no penalty required.

title: Women's RWC — France vs England thriller
explanation: The tackled player does not release the ball immediately after being brought to ground. Law 14: a tackled player must release the ball immediately and move away or get up. Penalty for holding on.

title: Women's RWC — clean try scoring
explanation: A well-worked try with clear grounding. The ball is placed on or over the try line with downward pressure. No infringement in the build-up. Award the try.

title: Black Ferns vs USA 2025 — tackle assessment
explanation: A solid tackle in women's international rugby. Good technique with arms wrapped below the shoulders. Physical but legal. Play on — not every big collision requires a whistle.

title: Black Ferns vs USA 2025 — breakdown infringement
explanation: A player goes off their feet over the ball at the ruck, preventing the opposition from competing. This is 'sealing off' — the player is not supporting their own body weight and is lying on or over the ball. Penalty.

title: Women's Test — England vs New Zealand 2024
explanation: The ball carrier loses the ball forward from their hands. A knock-on results in a scrum to the non-offending team. Simple but fundamental — the ball must travel forward from the hands or arms.

title: Women's 6N — England vs Scotland 2025 — no foul at ruck
explanation: Physical contest at the ruck but everything is legal. Players enter from behind the hindmost foot, stay on their feet, and compete fairly. Intense but legal — this is what good rucking looks like.

title: Premiership — Sale vs Gloucester — breakdown contest
explanation: Once a ruck has formed, players must not handle the ball — they must use their feet to move the ball. A player reaching into the ruck with their hands to play the ball is a penalty offence.

title: Premiership — Sale vs Gloucester — clean lineout
explanation: A well-executed lineout with no infringement. The throw is straight, lifters support legally, and the jumper catches cleanly. No penalty — this is how a lineout should work.

title: Premiership — Bath vs Saracens — offside at ruck
explanation: A defender advances beyond the offside line at the ruck. The offside line is the hindmost foot of the last player bound in the ruck. Clear penalty — the defender was a metre ahead of the line when they made the tackle.

title: Premiership — Bath vs Northampton — no foul in tackle
explanation: A dominant tackle that sends the ball carrier backwards. Looks dramatic but is completely legal — arms wrapped, contact below the shoulders, no dangerous technique. Don't penalise good defence.

title: Premiership — Leicester vs Bristol — scrum collapse
explanation: The scrum collapses. The referee must determine which side caused it — look for who is driving down, who is angling in, and whether the hooker is being impeded. Scrum decisions require experience and positioning.

title: Mass indiscipline — 16 yellows and 4 reds
explanation: In a game with historic levels of indiscipline (16 yellow cards and 4 red cards), the foul play is blatant. This clip shows a clear dangerous play incident with no mitigation possible.

title: Grassroots — Rugby St Andrews vs Northampton Casuals
explanation: A typical grassroots rugby passage of play with no infringement. The tackle is legal, the ball is released, and the ruck forms correctly. Good, clean community rugby. The laws apply the same at all levels.

title: Grassroots — tackle technique assessment
explanation: At grassroots level, tackle technique can be less refined. This tackle makes contact above the shoulders. The Head Contact Process applies at all levels of the game — penalty and the referee must assess the degree of danger for a possible card.

title: Grassroots — Oakham vs Rugby St Andrews — ruck infringement
explanation: At grassroots level, players often go off their feet at the ruck. A player arriving at the ruck must bind and stay on their feet. Going to ground over the ball prevents fair contest. Penalty — this is one of the most common infringements at community level.

title: Grassroots — Oakham vs Rugby St Andrews — clean play
explanation: A good passage of play at community level. Tackles are legal, ball is released, rucks form correctly. No whistle needed. At grassroots level, it's important to let the game flow when the laws are being respected.

title: Super Rugby Final — Crusaders vs Chiefs — forward pass debate
explanation: A pass that appears to travel forward relative to the passer. Remember: a forward pass is judged by the direction the ball travels out of the hands, not where it's caught (momentum can carry the ball forward after a backwards pass). This one is marginal — exactly the kind of decision referees debate.

title: Super Rugby Final — Crusaders vs Chiefs — no foul try
explanation: A well-worked try in the Super Rugby Final. Clear grounding with downward pressure, no infringement in the build-up. Award the try — don't look for problems that aren't there.

title: Forward pass example — law education clip
explanation: A clear forward pass example. The ball travels forward from the passer's hands towards the opposing dead-ball line. Scrum to the non-offending team. This is a fundamental law — no forward passing in rugby.

title: Offside at ruck — law education clip
explanation: An educational clip demonstrating offside at the ruck (Law 16.5d). Players not in the ruck must remain behind the hindmost foot of the last player in the ruck. Advancing beyond this line is offside — penalty.

title: All Blacks vs England 2024 Dunedin — legal contest
explanation: An intense, physical passage in a one-point thriller. Both teams competing hard at the breakdown but within the laws. The contest is fierce but legal — supports stay on feet, tacklers release, ball is available. Let the game breathe.

title: All Blacks vs England 2024 Twickenham — penalty and sin bin
explanation: In the dying moments at Twickenham, a breakdown penalty changes the game. Repeated infringements may warrant a yellow card even if individual offences are only penalties. The referee uses team warnings and manages the pattern of offending.
